items.question_text: Under 13-1-11, delay information may be automatically recorded via use of the ERAM Hold Data Menu, ERAM Hold View, a hold message, or manually on _____ or facility-approved worksheets.
items.explanation: 13-1-11(b) lists flight progress strips as the manual recording option alongside facility-approved worksheets. Both manual and automatic methods must follow the facility-defined standard.

choices.text: flight progress strips

items.question_text: Under 13-1-11 NOTE, when using the ERAM Hold Data Menu or Hold View, delays are automatically recorded when:
items.explanation: The note specifically states delays are automatically recorded when the aircraft is cleared out of hold, not upon entering holding. The auto-recording trigger is the exit event.

choices.text: As soon as the controller enters the aircraft into the Hold Data Menu.
When the aircraft reports entering the holding pattern.
When the aircraft is cleared out of hold.
At the top of each hour regardless of the aircraft's hold status.

items.question_text: The first operational duty listed under 13-1-11 is to adhere to all applicable _____.
items.explanation: 13-1-11(a) states 'Adhere to all applicable delay reporting directives.' This is the foundational requirement before the recording methods in (b).

choices.text: delay reporting directives

items.question_text: Under 13-1-12, upon receipt of the overdue aircraft notification, what actions must be taken?
items.explanation: 13-1-12 directs controllers to take appropriate actions set forth in Chapter 10, Section 3, Overdue aircraft. The paragraph cross-references another chapter rather than enumerating its own procedures.

choices.text: Treat the notification as a data-entry error and update the route of flight to remove it.
Take appropriate actions set forth in Chapter 10, Section 3, Overdue aircraft.
Initiate a communications search by calling the aircraft on all available frequencies.
Notify the operations supervisor and wait for further direction.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-12 NOTE, updating an aircraft's route of flight will remove the overdue aircraft notification. Does this action alone satisfy the overdue aircraft response requirements?
items.explanation: The body of the paragraph requires taking overdue aircraft actions per Chapter 10 Section 3. The note describes a technical fact about how the notification can be cleared (route update), but this does not replace the required procedural response.

choices.text: Yes. Once the notification is removed by updating the route, no further action is required.
Yes, but only if the route update corrects an actual error in the flight plan.
No. The notification triggers overdue aircraft actions under Chapter 10, Section 3. Clearing the notification by updating the route does not replace the required actions.
No, but only if the aircraft has also failed to make a position report within the last 15 minutes.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-13, graphic depictions of flight trajectories may be used only to aid in _____ and strategic planning.
items.explanation: 13-1-13(a) limits GPD use to situational awareness and strategic planning. This is the only authorized use; all other operational applications are prohibited.

choices.text: situational awareness

items.question_text: Under 13-1-13(d), which of the following uses of the GPD is prohibited?
items.explanation: 13-1-13(d) prohibits GPD use for radar identification, position information, transfer of radar identification, radar separation, correlation, or point outs. Monitoring a trajectory for situational awareness is the only authorized use.

choices.text: Using the GPD to monitor expected trajectories as part of strategic planning.
Using the GPD for radar separation.
Using the GPD to anticipate potential conflict zones for workload management.
Using the GPD to assess whether a reroute would keep an aircraft clear of SUA.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-13, trajectory-based altitude must not be used in lieu of _____ for altitude confirmation.
items.explanation: 13-1-13(c) explicitly prohibits using trajectory-based altitude as a substitute for Mode C for altitude confirmation. Mode C (transponder altitude reporting) is the required source.

choices.text: Mode C

items.question_text: Under 13-1-14, when current forecast wind data are not available, what must controllers continue to do, and with what caveat?
items.explanation: 13-1-14 requires continued use of conflict probe and trial planning with appropriate recognition that alert and trajectory data may be affected. The tools are not discontinued; they are used with heightened awareness.

choices.text: Suspend conflict probe but continue trial planning, because trial planning is less dependent on wind data.
Continue using conflict probe and trial planning with appropriate recognition that alert and trajectory data may be affected.
Continue both tools without any special recognition, because forecast winds are not a primary input to either system.
Suspend both tools and revert to manual conflict prediction until forecast wind data are restored.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-14, what specific data types may be affected when current forecast wind data are unavailable?
items.explanation: The paragraph names two specific outputs that may be affected: alert data and trajectory data. Controllers must recognize the impact on both when forecast winds are unavailable.

choices.text: alert and trajectory data

items.question_text: Under 13-1-15, when there is a loss of connectivity to an adjacent ERAM facility, what must happen to EDST operations and what warning must be observed?
items.explanation: 13-1-15 mirrors the same pattern as 13-1-14: continue use, but with recognition that alert data may be affected. The tool is not disabled by the connectivity loss.

choices.text: Suspend all EDST use until connectivity is restored, because the system cannot function without adjacent facility data.
Continue use of EDST with appropriate recognition that alert data may be affected.
Continue EDST use without qualification, because only local sector data affects alerting.
Switch to an offline EDST mode that operates using only forecast data until connectivity returns.

items.situation: A flight is in holding using the ERAM Hold View. When the aircraft is cleared out of hold, another controller assumes the delay was manually recorded on a strip and does not verify whether the Hold View auto-recording captured it. A third controller says delay recording is optional because the facility has no specific TMU request for this flight's delay data.
items.question_text: How should delay information be handled for this holding aircraft?

choices.text: Skip delay recording entirely for this flight since TMU has not specifically requested the information.
Manually record the delay on a strip because Hold View auto-recording cannot be relied upon for official delay reporting.

Recognize that Hold View automatically records the delay when the aircraft is cleared out of hold. Delay information must be recorded, and facility delay reporting directives must be followed regardless of whether TMU requests the data.
Record the delay only if the total holding time exceeds 10 minutes, because shorter delays are not operationally significant.

items.explanation: 13-1-11 Requires adherence to applicable delay reporting directives and recording of delay information. The Hold Data Menu or Hold View auto-records when the aircraft is cleared out of hold. Delay recording is mandatory, not conditional on TMU requests.

items.question_text: An overdue aircraft notification is based on radar track data. A controller updates the aircraft's route of flight and the notification clears. What else must the controller do?

choices.text: Nothing additional is needed. Clearing the notification by updating the route of flight resolves the situation.
Notify the operations supervisor that the notification was a false alarm caused by stale route data.
Take the overdue aircraft actions required by Chapter 10, Section 3, because the notification was received, regardless of whether updating the route cleared the alert.
Monitor the aircraft for five additional minutes to confirm the notification does not reappear.

items.explanation: 13-1-12 Says 'Upon receipt of the overdue aircraft notification take appropriate actions set forth in Chapter 10, Section 3.' The notification itself triggers the response, regardless of whether the subsequent route update clears the alert from the display.

items.situation: A sector team is using the GPD to assess whether two aircraft will lose separation in 12 minutes based on their predicted trajectories. One controller suggests using the trajectory altitude instead of Mode C to verify one aircraft's altitude since the Mode C readout is intermittent. Another says the GPD is fine for assessing the future conflict picture but cannot replace Mode C for current altitude verification.

choices.text: Using the GPD to assess a potential future conflict as part of strategic planning.
Using the GPD to identify where additional radar monitoring might be needed.
Using the GPD trajectory-based altitude in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation.
Using the GPD to evaluate whether a proposed altitude change would clear a crossing conflict.

items.explanation: 13-1-13(C) explicitly prohibits using trajectory-based altitude in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation, even when the Mode C readout is intermittent. The other uses are appropriate situational awareness and strategic planning activities.

items.situation: Current forecast wind data are unavailable. One controller wants to suspend all conflict probe and trial planning immediately, saying the tools are unreliable without wind data. Another wants to treat all alerts as fully accurate, because if the conflict probe generated an alert it must still be valid even without wind data.
items.question_text: What is the correct approach under 13-1-14?

choices.text: Suspend both tools until forecast wind data are restored, because neither can function reliably without wind data.
Continue use of conflict probe and trial planning, but recognize that the unavailability of current forecast wind data means alert and trajectory data may be affected.
Continue using both tools with no special recognition, because forecast wind loss does not affect automated alerting or trajectory predictions.
Use trial planning only and suspend conflict probe, because trial planning calculations do not depend on wind data.

items.explanation: 13-1-14 Requires continued use of both tools with the explicit caveat that alert and trajectory data may be affected. Neither suspension nor unqualified use is correct.

items.question_text: How does the operational response to loss of interfacility connectivity (13-1-15) compare to the response to loss of forecast winds (13-1-14)?

choices.text: Forecast wind loss requires complete suspension of tools; connectivity loss allows continued use with no special recognition.

Both require continued use of the affected tools with recognition that certain data may be affected. For forecast wind loss, both alert AND trajectory data are affected. For connectivity loss, alert data may be affected.
Both require immediate suspension of all EDST functions until the data source is restored.
There is no operational difference; both paragraphs give identical guidance.

items.explanation: Both 13-1-14 and 13-1-15 follow a 'continue use with recognition' pattern, but the affected data differs: wind loss may affect both alert and trajectory data; connectivity loss may affect alert data specifically. Neither requires suspension.
